request.question: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?

title: Genadendal Residence
Government House, Jersey
Eastcliff (mansion)
Government House, Bermuda
President of Germany
State House (Kenya)
Politics of Berlin
Palais Schaumburg
Presidential palace
Hammerschmidt Villa
sentences: Genadendal Residence ] is the official residence of the President of South Africa when in office at Tuynhuys, Cape Town.
Genadendal is situated in Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa.
It has been the official residence since 1994 when Nelson Mandela took up residence here rather than neighbouring Groote Schuur.
The building, formerly known as Westbrook, is named after the town of Genadendal – itself an Afrikaans word meaning "Valley of Mercy".
Government House is the official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey.
The building is situated in the parish of St Saviour in Jersey.
It is also used for ceremonial functions, receptions and meetings with visiting foreign dignitaries and heads of state.
It is also the official residence of the Duke of Normandy (currently Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom) as head of state when staying in Jersey.
Eastcliff is a 20-room house overlooking the Mississippi River in St. Paul, Minnesota, which serves as the official residence of the president of the University of Minnesota system.

It was first built in 1922 by local lumber magnate Edward Brooks Sr. and donated to the university by the Brooks family in 1958, beginning its service as the president's official residence in 1960 when O. Meredith Wilson took the position.
In 2000, the home was added to the National Register of Historic Places.
Today, it is the most-visited public residence in the state (outpacing the Minnesota Governor's Residence), often hosting events five days a week.
6,800 people were recorded attending 159 events in 2005.
Government House is the official residence of the Governor of Bermuda.
It is located on Langton Hill, overlooking the North Shore in Hamilton, Pembroke.
Government House is also the official residence of the Bermudian head of state (currently Queen Elizabeth II) when staying in Bermuda.
The President of Germany, officially the President of the Federal Republic of Germany (German: "Bundespräsident der Bundesrepublik Deutschland" ), is the head of state of Germany.
State House is the official residence of the President of Kenya.
It was the residence of the Prime Minister of Kenya from independence until Kenya (12 Dec 1963) transformed into a republic (12 Dec 1964).
As the Prime Minister's position was abolished, it has been the official residence of the president since then.
Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany.
The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue.
Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt").
Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998.
The Bundesrat ("federal council", functioning as an upper house) represents the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian House of Lords.

Palais Schaumburg is a neoclassical-style building in Bonn, Germany, which served as the primary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the primary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1949 until 1999.
As the headquarters of the Federal Chancellery, it was simply known as "the House of the Federal Chancellor" (German: "Haus des Bundeskanzlers" ).
Since 1999, Palais Schaumburg has served as the secondary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the secondary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany.
A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.
However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.
Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.
The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.
President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.
Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.
In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?
hop1_docs: Official residence | An official residence is the residence at which a nation's head of state, head of government, governor or other senior figure officially resides.  It may or may not be the same location where the individual conducts work-related functions or lives.

Royal Palace of Brussels | The Royal Palace of Brussels (Dutch: "Koninklijk Paleis van Brussel" ] , French: "Palais Royal de Bruxelles" , German: "Königlicher Palast von Brüssel" ) is the official palace of the King and Queen of the Belgians in the centre of the nation's capital Brussels.  However it is not used as a royal residence, as the king and his family live in the Royal Palace of Laeken on the outskirts of Brussels.  The website of the Belgian Monarchy describes the function of the palace as follows: ""The Palace is where His Majesty the King exercises his prerogatives as Head of State, grants audiences and deals with affairs of state.  Apart from the offices of the King and the Queen, the Royal Palace houses the services of the Grand Marshal of the Court, the King's Head of Cabinet, the Head of the King's Military Household and the Intendant of the King's Civil List.  The Palace also includes the State Rooms where large receptions are held, as well as the apartments provided for foreign Heads of State during official visits.""

Government House, Jersey | Government House is the official residence of the Lieutenant Governor of Jersey.  The building is situated in the parish of St Saviour in Jersey.  It is also used for ceremonial functions, receptions and meetings with visiting foreign dignitaries and heads of state.  It is also the official residence of the Duke of Normandy (currently Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom) as head of state when staying in Jersey.

Presidential palace | A presidential palace is the official residence of the president in some countries.  However, some countries do not call the official residence of their head of state a "palace" or use the building only as a workplace separate from the president's actual home.  Some presidential palaces were once the official residences to monarchs in former monarchies that were preserved during those states' transition into republics.  The Cumhurbaşkanlığı Sarayı in the Turkey is the world's largest Presidential Palace, four times that of Versailles.

Hammerschmidt Villa | Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.  President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.  Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.  In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.
The Residency, Penang | The Residency in George Town, Penang, now formally named Seri Mutiara, is the official residence of the "Yang di-Pertua Negeri" (The Head of The State) of Penang.

Grand Ducal Palace, Luxembourg | The Grand Ducal Palace (Luxembourgish: "Groussherzogleche Palais" , French: "Palais grand-ducal" , German: "Großherzogliches Palais" ) is a palace in Luxembourg City, in southern Luxembourg.  It is the official residence of the Grand Duke of Luxembourg, and where he performs most of his duties as head of state of the Grand Duchy.
response.summary_1:
Official residences for the President of the Federal Republic of Germany

- "Hammerschmidt Villa" — "served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany" "from 1950 until 1994." (passage [5])
- "President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994." (passage [5]) 
- "Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President." (passage [5])

Ambiguities / missing facts:
- The passages refer to the "President of the Federal Republic of Germany" but do not explicitly use the phrase "head of state"; missing fact: explicit statement linking "head of state" → "President of the Federal Republic of Germany" (bridge entity: President of the Federal Republic of Germany).
- Multiple residences noted (primary vs secondary) — need to confirm which is the current official residence location.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. "Bellevue Palace" (Schloss Bellevue) — article lede/infobox (confirm "official residence" label and Berlin location).
2. "President of the Federal Republic of Germany" — article lede/infobox (confirm role = head of state and official residence field).
3. "Villa Hammerschmidt" (Villa Hammerschmidt) — lede/infobox (confirm Bonn location and secondary-residence status).
4. If needed: search keywords "official residence head of state Germany Bellevue Palace Berlin official residence".
response.hop2_query: President of the Federal Republic of Germany official residence location
hop2_docs: Hammerschmidt Villa | Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994.  President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994.  Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the President.  In German, the Villa is also called the "White House of Bonn", because of its vague resemblance to the official residence of the President of the United States.

Palais Schaumburg | Palais Schaumburg is a neoclassical-style building in Bonn, Germany, which served as the primary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the primary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1949 until 1999.  As the headquarters of the Federal Chancellery, it was simply known as "the House of the Federal Chancellor" (German: "Haus des Bundeskanzlers" ).  Since 1999, Palais Schaumburg has served as the secondary official seat of the German Federal Chancellery and the secondary official residence of the Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany.

President of Germany | The President of Germany, officially the President of the Federal Republic of Germany (German: "Bundespräsident der Bundesrepublik Deutschland" ), is the head of state of Germany.

Politics of Berlin | Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany.  The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue.  Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt").  Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998.  The Bundesrat ("federal council", functioning as an upper house) represents the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian House of Lords.
Official Residence of the President (Republic of China) | Official Residence of the President (Republic of China)

President's Pavilion | President's Pavilion is an official residence of the President of Sri Lanka, located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.  This was formerly the Governor's Pavilion until 1972 when Sri Lanka became a republic.  It is located close to the sacred Temple of the Tooth in the center of the city of Kandy at the head of the Pavilion Street.  Although an official residence, it is used rarely for official functions, most notably during the Esala Perahera.

Shilin Official Residence | The Shilin Official Residence () is the former residence of late Republic of China President Chiang Kai-shek located on Zhongshan North Road in Shilin District, Taipei, Taiwan.
hop2_titles: Official Residence of the President (Republic of China)
response.summary_2: President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994. The President of the Federal Republic of Germany is the head of state, and the office's official residence is Schloss Bellevue (Bellevue Palace) in Berlin, while Villa Hammerschmidt in Bonn has served as a secondary official residence since 1994.
response.answer: The official residence of Germany's head of state is Schloss Bellevue (Bellevue Palace) in Berlin. Villa Hammerschmidt in Bonn has served as a secondary official residence since 1994.
